comments.text: This conjecture has been proved by P. Frankl, H. Lu, J. Ma and Y. Wu in [FLMW26], when $r=4$ and $n \geq 5(k-1)$.
A complete proof of this conjecture has been "claimed" by T. K. Mishra in this latest preprint: [Mi26].

I have contacted the author this morning. Page 11, II: A bad set becomes good This is not necessarily the case as written, so some additional argument would be needed. (the first shifts might not be possible since the shifted set(s) belong(s) already to the family, so one should conclude that at the end, we have a so-called trivial family, which doesnt seem to be obvious) The first pages contain results which are known for quite a while. I.e., one knew already that it was sufficient to consider shifted families. update: I will have a closer look in the coming days on the paper.

Found the crucial mistake: The mistake happens in Lemma 4 (which is referred to in the updated arxiv version when dealing with page 11, II, bad -> good), case 2, and actually already in line 4 of the proof of Lemma 4 when used later. If $F \in \mathcal F^{i_t}$, it is not that for all $1 \le k \le r$ we have $\mathcal C_{i_kj_k}(F)=F$. A simple counterexample for the whole thing (the algorithm in section 3) can be obtained with $n=6, k=2, s=3$ and $\mathcal F= \{12,13,14,15,16,23,24,34\},$ where $A=34$ and $B=25$ or $26$. ($ab$ represents the set $\{a,b\}$)

This conjecture has been solved in affirmative for $r=3$ and large enough $n$ (where, $n =$ vertices) by T. Luczak and K. Mieczkowska in [LuMi14]. Whereas, the result of [HLS12] in the discussion above has been improved by P. Frankl, T. Luczak and K. Mieczkowska in [FLM12]. [FLM12]: P. Frankl, T. Łuczak, K. Mieczkowska, On matchings in hypergraphs, Electron. J. Combin. 19 (2)(2012), P42. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
